claim: MCP servers consume 5-10K tokens before agents start working, often representing 5-10% of total context window even for small servers
actionable: Audit your MCP server context consumption; for context-critical agents, explore CLI-based tools or script-based approaches instead of full MCP servers
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: CLI-based tool approach: teach agents how to use CLI by providing a concise readme and CLI interface definition (~25 lines) instead of MCP server schema
actionable: Create a CLI tool wrapper with a readme describing workflows; prime your agent with a brief prompt to read only those two files and ignore other code
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: CLI approach reduces context overhead by ~50-60% compared to full MCP servers while maintaining same functionality and agent control
actionable: Use CLI-based tools when you need 50%+ context savings and full control over what the agent can access; trade off simplicity for control
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Script-based approach with progressive disclosure: only load script names and high-level descriptions via readme; agents call '--help' on scripts as needed
actionable: Structure tools as self-contained single-file scripts with '--help' documentation; prime agents with a readme that lists when to use each script
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Script-based approach reduces context to <2K tokens (~1% of agent window) versus 10K tokens for MCP servers, achieving 90%+ token savings
actionable: For context-limited agents, use scripts with progressive disclosure; agents dynamically load script details only when needed via '--help'
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Anthropic recommends wrapping specific CLI functionality in MCP servers with direct tool calls instead of exposing all capabilities to agents
actionable: If using MCP servers, expose only the specific functionality agents need via individual tools rather than full CLI interfaces
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Skills (Claude ecosystem) and scripts both enable progressive disclosure but skills are locked into Claude ecosystem while scripts are portable and tool-agnostic
actionable: Use skills for Claude-exclusive projects requiring progressive disclosure; use scripts for portability across agents and teams
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Recommended approach: 80% of time use CLI for new tools, 10% use MCP for multi-agent scale scenarios, 10% use scripts/skills for extreme context preservation
actionable: Start with CLI tools; only move to MCP servers if scaling to multiple agents; use scripts/skills only if context is critically constrained
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Build CLI tools first, then wrap them in MCP servers if needed; this provides CLI interoperability, portability, and fallback to simpler approaches
actionable: Develop CLI interfaces before creating MCP servers; ensure all MCP tools delegate to underlying CLI commands for consistency and flexibility
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: Prompt engineering to teach agents CLI usage (5-line basic prompt) is more effective at controlling context than relying on tool schema descriptions
actionable: Use minimal prompts: 'Read these files, summarize tools, call them as needed'—this gives agents full control with 50-90% less context overhead
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?

claim: MCP servers offer simplicity and standardization; CLI/script approaches require more engineering but provide full control and context efficiency
actionable: Trade off complexity for control: choose MCP for convenience, CLI for flexibility, scripts for extreme context optimization
source_title: Why are top engineers DITCHING MCP Servers?
